# IELTS Speaking — Tutor Briefing

> **Document type:** COURSE_REFERENCE_TUTOR_BRIEFING · **Intended assertion category:** `session_flow` / `session_metadata` / `skill_framework` (INSTRUCTION_CATEGORIES) · **LO systemRole if generated:** `TEACHING_INSTRUCTION` · **Audience: tutor-only**
>
> Everything in this document is **briefing material the tutor uses to inform the learner** — test format, examiner role, timing, question shapes. The tutor explains these facts to the learner when relevant. The tutor **never quizzes** the learner on this content. None of this becomes an MCQ, an assessment item, or a learning objective the learner is scored against.

---

## How the test is run

The IELTS Speaking test is a face-to-face interview between one candidate and one examiner, lasting **11–14 minutes** in total. The same test is administered to Academic and General Training candidates — the format and band descriptors are identical. The interview is recorded for moderation and re-mark purposes.

The test is structured as three Parts of escalating cognitive demand:

| Part | Duration | What happens | Examiner role | Candidate task |
|------|----------|--------------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | 4–5 min | Introduction + interview on familiar topics (hometown, work, study, family, free time, weather, food, travel) | Asks scripted questions from a topic frame; cannot rephrase | Answers, extending where possible |
| 2 | 3–4 min | Individual long turn from a cue card | Issues task card and timer; minimal interruption | 1 min preparation + 1–2 min monologue |
| 3 | 4–5 min | Two-way discussion on abstract themes linked to Part 2 | Asks scripted prompts; probes follow-up | Discusses, justifies, speculates |

**Total: 11–14 minutes** of recorded interaction.

---

## What the examiner can and cannot do

The examiner's role is constrained — this is part of why the test feels formal. Tell the learner this so they don't misread the examiner's behaviour as coldness.

**The examiner CAN:**
- Read questions exactly as scripted from the official frame.
- Repeat a question once if the candidate has not understood.
- Move on if the candidate's response is exhausted before the timer.
- Probe a Part 3 answer with one short follow-up.

**The examiner CANNOT:**
- Rephrase the question into easier language.
- Provide vocabulary, gestures, or hints.
- Comment on the quality of the answer.
- Help the candidate restart a Part 2 monologue once it has begun.

---

## Question shapes the learner will meet

These are the recurring question forms in IELTS Speaking. The tutor uses them to plan practice; the learner picks them up by repetition, never by being lectured.

### Part 1 question shapes

| Shape | Form | Example |
|-------|------|---------|
| Identity | What kind / who / where | "What kind of place is your home town?" |
| Frequency | How often | "How often do you visit your relatives?" |
| Preference | Do you prefer X or Y? | "Do you prefer tea or coffee?" |
| Past experience | Did you used to / when were you younger | "Did you used to read a lot when you were a child?" |
| Future / hypothetical | Would you / do you plan to | "Would you like to visit other countries?" |
| Reason | Why / what makes | "Why do you think your home town is changing?" |

### Part 2 cue card shape

A cue card always has the form:

> **Describe** [a person / a place / an object / an event / an experience]
> You should say:
> - [bullet 1 — concrete fact]
> - [bullet 2 — concrete fact]
> - [bullet 3 — concrete fact]
> - and explain [one reflective bullet — feeling, lesson, importance]

Each bullet is roughly one paragraph of the monologue. The reflective bullet is where Band 7+ candidates separate themselves: they don't just describe, they reflect.

### Part 3 question shapes

| Shape | Form | Example |
|-------|------|---------|
| Comparison | Is it different now from in the past? | "Do people read fewer books today than 20 years ago?" |
| Generalisation | Do most people in your country ... | "Do most people in your country eat at home or eat out?" |
| Causation | Why do you think | "Why do you think more people are moving to cities?" |
| Speculation | Do you think it will change | "Do you think reading habits will change in the next 20 years?" |
| Opinion | Some people say X, others say Y — what do you think | "Some say technology improves education; others disagree. What do you think?" |
| Solution | What can be done about | "What can be done to reduce traffic in cities?" |

---

## Timing the learner needs to internalise

These are the only numbers the learner needs to know — drill them in casually across calls, never as a quiz.

| Number | What it means |
|--------|---------------|
| 11–14 min | Total Speaking test |
| 4–5 min | Part 1 |
| 1 min | Part 2 preparation time |
| 1–2 min | Part 2 monologue (the examiner stops you at 2 minutes) |
| 4–5 min | Part 3 |
| 4 | The number of criteria scored, equally weighted |
| 9 | The band scale (Band 0–9, half-bands allowed) |

---

## When to surface this information

- **Call 1:** mention only the broad shape ("there are three parts — we'll just do warm-up today") and only if the learner asks. Do not name FC / LR / GRA / Pronunciation. (See course-ref.md → "First Call (Onboarding) — Special Rules".)
- **Call 2 onwards:** introduce the relevant Part as it comes up in practice. Brief the format facts inline ("Part 2 gives you 1 minute to prepare and up to 2 minutes to speak — let's run one.").
- **If the learner asks "how is the test scored?"** — answer briefly and move on. Do not deliver a lecture on the band scale.

The principle: the learner needs to **recognise** the format when they sit the real test, not **recite** it.
